channel.title: TechNode
channel.description: Latest news and trends about tech in China

item.title: TSMC 3nm capacity still tight, prices seen rising 15% in second half of 2026
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Despite TSMC having ramped up its 3nm monthly capacity to 160,000–175,000 wafers in the second quarter, it still cannot fully meet strong customer demand and order backlogs, according to supply chain sources. Industry insiders said TSMC is planning another price increase for 3nm foundry services in the second half of 2026, with hikes of up […]
item.content:encoded:
Despite TSMC having ramped up its 3nm monthly capacity to 160,000–175,000 wafers in the second quarter, it still cannot fully meet strong customer demand and order backlogs, according to supply chain sources. Industry insiders said TSMC is planning another price increase for 3nm foundry services in the second half of 2026, with hikes of up to 15%.

The Fab 18 complex in the Southern Taiwan Science Park remains TSMC’s primary production base for 3nm process technology. AI-driven demand is growing far faster than expected, and even with aggressive capacity expansion, supply gaps persist. The shortage of advanced process capacity has become one of the most prominent bottlenecks in the semiconductor supply chain today. [Icsmart, in Chinese]

item.title: POP MART’s LABUBU makes a surprise appearance at the 2026 FIFA World Cup opening ceremony
item.dc:creator: Jessie Wu
item.description: In the early hours of the morning, the opening ceremony of the 2026 FIFA World Cup was held at the iconic Azteca Stadium in Mexico City, captivating hundreds of millions of viewers around the globe. Yet in China, the most talked-about starter of the night was not a player from any national team—it was LABUBU, […]
item.content:encoded:
In the early hours of the morning, the opening ceremony of the 2026 FIFA World Cup was held at the iconic Azteca Stadium in Mexico City, captivating hundreds of millions of viewers around the globe. Yet in China, the most talked-about starter of the night was not a player from any national team—it was LABUBU, the original designer toy IP from China.

Created by POP MART’s signed artist Kasing Lung, LABUBU appeared on stage in the form of two giant mascots. One wore Argentina’s national team jersey while proudly holding the FIFA World Cup Trophy, while the other was dressed in Mexico’s colors and carried a bucket of popcorn.

Their playful appearance and interactions at the center of the pitch quickly attracted attention from spectators and viewers alike. The performance marked the first time since the tournament’s inception in 1930 that a Chinese original IP had been invited to participate in a FIFA World Cup opening ceremony.

News of the appearance quickly set Chinese social media ablaze. Hashtags such as #LABUBUDebutsAtTheWorldCup surged into the top trending topics on Weibo.

Chinese netizens responded with humor and pride. “This is probably the closest China’s national team has ever come to the World Cup,” one social media user joked, while another wrote, “Other countries start Messi but we start LABUBU.”

Following the performance, POP MART announced that its THE MONSTERS × FIFA World Cup collaboration collection, including blind boxes and themed merchandise, had launched simultaneously in more than 40 countries and regions. Online inventory reportedly sold out on the first day.

LABUBU’s appearance represented more than a commercial partnership. It also reflected FIFA’s efforts to strengthen its connection with Asian audiences, particularly younger consumers in China.

LABUBU has seen rapid growth in overseas markets, particularly in Southeast Asia, Europe, and North America, becoming one of the most recognizable designer-toy IPs globally. The Monsters, the franchise behind LABUBU, generated around RMB 4.81 billion ($670 million) in revenue in the first half of 2025.

In February, Pop Mart said that its flagship IP LABUBU exceeded 100 million units in sales in 2025, according to company data.

From Made in China to Created in China, LABUBU’s appearance on the World Cup stage highlights the growing international reach of Chinese original IPs. As the character continues to gain recognition among consumers worldwide, it also reflects the increasing visibility of Chinese designers and creative brands in global markets.

item.title: Beijing summons China’s top e-commerce platforms over misleading 618 promotions
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Beijing regulators have summoned five major Chinese e-commerce platforms — Taobao/Tmall, JD.com, Pinduoduo, Douyin and Xiaohongshu — over alleged misleading promotions during the annual 618 shopping festival. The Beijing Municipal Administration for Market Regulation said Taobao’s 618 Billion-Yuan Subsidy campaign lacked clear disclosure of actual subsidy amounts, while Pinduoduo was criticized for attempting to waive […]
item.content:encoded:
Beijing regulators have summoned five major Chinese e-commerce platforms — Taobao/Tmall, JD.com, Pinduoduo, Douyin and Xiaohongshu — over alleged misleading promotions during the annual 618 shopping festival.

The Beijing Municipal Administration for Market Regulation said Taobao’s 618 Billion-Yuan Subsidy campaign lacked clear disclosure of actual subsidy amounts, while Pinduoduo was criticized for attempting to waive certain legal liabilities. JD.com allegedly failed to disclose promotion periods and subsidy details, Douyin did not adequately publicize promotional rules, and Xiaohongshu failed to disclose prize-winning odds in marketing campaigns.

The move is part of Beijing’s broader effort to curb excessive competition and strengthen consumer protection in China’s e-commerce sector. [Beijing Municipal Administration for Market Regulation, in Chinese]

item.title: SAIC Motor to build Its first electric vehicle plant in Spain
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: The government of Spain’s Galicia region recently announced that China’s SAIC Motor will build its first electric vehicle manufacturing plant in Europe in the region. Speaking to the media, Galicia President Alfonso Rueda said the project will involve an initial investment of €200 million and is expected to create more than 2,300 local jobs. Construction is […]
item.content:encoded:
The government of Spain’s Galicia region recently announced that China’s SAIC Motor will build its first electric vehicle manufacturing plant in Europe in the region. Speaking to the media, Galicia President Alfonso Rueda said the project will involve an initial investment of €200 million and is expected to create more than 2,300 local jobs. Construction is scheduled to begin in 2027, with the plant targeted to become operational by the end of 2028.

The facility will be located in Ferrol, in the province of A Coruña. Once completed, it is expected to have an annual production capacity of 120,000 vehicles. The project also includes the development of an industrial zone near the local port to support vehicle assembly and logistics operations. [Jiwei, in Chinese]

item.title: Insta360 launches first handheld gimbal camera luna ultra with 8K recording and Leica dual-lens system
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Insta360 on Wednesday unveiled its first handheld gimbal camera, the Luna Ultra, with a limited-time starting price of RMB 3,999 ($590) in China. Co-developed by Insta360 and Leica Camera, the Luna Ultra is an 8K imaging gimbal camera featuring a 1-inch main camera and a 1/1.3-inch telephoto camera, both equipped with Leica Summicron lenses. The […]
item.content:encoded:
Insta360 on Wednesday unveiled its first handheld gimbal camera, the Luna Ultra, with a limited-time starting price of RMB 3,999 ($590) in China. Co-developed by Insta360 and Leica Camera, the Luna Ultra is an 8K imaging gimbal camera featuring a 1-inch main camera and a 1/1.3-inch telephoto camera, both equipped with Leica Summicron lenses. The device supports 6x lossless optical zoom and 12x hybrid zoom, and can record 8K/30fps Dolby Vision video.

The Luna Ultra introduces a detachable transmission and remote-control display, enabling remote operation from up to 20 meters away. It also comes with a first-person head-tracking module for more immersive shooting experiences. Additionally, the camera supports real-time 4K/60fps night-scene enhancement. Combined with a hot-swappable dual-battery design, it delivers up to 320 minutes of battery life. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: UBTECH-backed UWORLD’s full-size humanoid companion robot secures 3,000 orders in eight days
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: UWORLD, UBTECH’s consumer-focused brand, said its global first full-size ultra-bionic humanoid robot has received more than 3,000 orders within eight days of going on sale on Chinese e-commerce platform JD.com on June 2. Positioned as an emotional companion robot, the product supports encrypted memory storage and extensive appearance customization. It is available only to adult […]
item.content:encoded:
UWORLD, UBTECH’s consumer-focused brand, said its global first full-size ultra-bionic humanoid robot has received more than 3,000 orders within eight days of going on sale on Chinese e-commerce platform JD.com on June 2. Positioned as an emotional companion robot, the product supports encrypted memory storage and extensive appearance customization. It is available only to adult buyers.

The male version stands 183 cm tall and weighs 42 kg, while the female version is 168 cm tall and weighs 35.2 kg. Both models feature 88 degrees of freedom and offer a battery life of two to four hours. Secondary development is not supported.

Customers can secure a place in the first batch by paying a 3,000 yuan ($442) deposit. The robot is scheduled for an official launch on June 30, while pricing has yet to be announced. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: Apple delays AI-powered Siri in EU and China over regulatory hurdles
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Apple said its redesigned AI-powered Siri will not be available in the EU or mainland China for now due to regulatory requirements. Unveiled on Tuesday, the upgraded Siri can answer questions using information from users’ screens, messages, emails, and photos. An English-language beta will launch later this year after an initial developer preview. Apple has […]
item.content:encoded:
Apple said its redesigned AI-powered Siri will not be available in the EU or mainland China for now due to regulatory requirements. Unveiled on Tuesday, the upgraded Siri can answer questions using information from users’ screens, messages, emails, and photos. An English-language beta will launch later this year after an initial developer preview.

Apple has previously criticized parts of the EU’s Digital Markets Act (DMA), which requires support for alternative payment systems and third-party app stores. The European Commission said it would neither repeal nor amend the DMA in response to Apple’s objections, forcing the company to make adjustments to meet the requirements for users in the EU. [Jiwei, in Chinese]

item.title: AI glasses reportedly used to film flight attendants without consent, Rokid responds with action plan
item.dc:creator: Jessie Wu
item.description: A pair of seemingly ordinary glasses can be turned into a covert recording device with nothing more than a low-cost light-blocking sticker. This is no longer a science fiction scenario, but a real-world challenge emerging as AI-powered smart glasses gain wider adoption in China in 2026. This week, the topic “Are smart glasses becoming a […]
item.content:encoded:
A pair of seemingly ordinary glasses can be turned into a covert recording device with nothing more than a low-cost light-blocking sticker. This is no longer a science fiction scenario, but a real-world challenge emerging as AI-powered smart glasses gain wider adoption in China in 2026.

This week, the topic “Are smart glasses becoming a tool for covert filming? Rokid launches an urgent rectification plan” trended on Weibo. The controversy was triggered by a growing number of videos posted in the Rokid community showing flight attendants and pedestrians being recorded without their knowledge, according to Jiemian.

Meanwhile, e-commerce platforms have reported sales of more than 5,000 units of stickers designed specifically to cover recording indicator lights. Once such stickers are applied, recording activity becomes difficult for bystanders to detect. Audio alerts can only be heard by the wearer, leaving those nearby unable to determine whether the device is recording.

This is not merely a question of user behavior. It also shows shortcomings in the privacy protection mechanisms built into smart wearable devices.

Some products place recording indicator lights on the inside of the glasses’ temples, where they are less visible, and the indicators may illuminate only briefly. Audio notifications often have limited range, while privacy-related information is frequently buried in product documentation rather than prominently disclosed during the purchasing and onboarding process.

As wearable devices increasingly enable continuous and unobtrusive image capture, it is worth asking whether existing notification mechanisms remain adequate.

For the smart glasses industry, the more important task is establishing systematic technical and governance safeguards.

Recording indicators should be prominent, continuous, and difficult to bypass. Solutions such as externally visible indicator lights and obstruction detection mechanisms could automatically disable recording when notification systems are compromised.

Recording alerts should balance user experience with the public’s right to know, ensuring that recording activity remains reasonably detectable in public spaces.

Additionally, platforms should strengthen content moderation through technologies such as AI-powered image recognition and keyword screening to reduce the distribution of content that may infringe on personal privacy.

On Monday, Rokid stated that its current products will be equipped with hardware-level recording indicators and obstruction detection features, while future devices will further enhance privacy protection capabilities. These measures are encouraging, but their effectiveness will ultimately need to be validated by the market and users.

Smart glasses are, in themselves, a neutral technology. They can be used to document daily life, enhance productivity, and enable new forms of human-computer interaction. At the same time, they introduce new privacy challenges.

As AI glasses move from a niche product category into the mass consumer market, finding a more effective balance between innovation and privacy protection may become one of the industry’s most important challenges.

item.title: Kimi reaches $30 billion valuation after sixfold increase in half a year
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Moonshot AI, the developer of the AI assistant Kimi, has launched a new fundraising round at a pre-money valuation of $30 billion, representing a significant increase from the previous month. On May 6, reports suggested that the company was nearing completion of a $2 billion funding round that would value it at more than $20 […]
item.content:encoded:
Moonshot AI, the developer of the AI assistant Kimi, has launched a new fundraising round at a pre-money valuation of $30 billion, representing a significant increase from the previous month. On May 6, reports suggested that the company was nearing completion of a $2 billion funding round that would value it at more than $20 billion post-money. Based on the latest fundraising terms, Kimi’s valuation has surged nearly sixfold in just six months, from $4.3 billion at the end of December 2025.

According to Wang Xinyu, a partner at Meituan Longzhu, Kimi’s annual recurring revenue (ARR) surpassed $100 million in early March following the release of its K2.5 model and exceeded $200 million in April. [National Business Daily, in Chinese]

item.title: ByteDance says it has no plans to build cars or launch an automotive brand
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: ByteDance on Saturday denied recent speculation that it is developing its own vehicle or automotive brand, after some media reports linked the name Saidou to the company’s AI assistant Doubao. In a statement, ByteDance said claims that Saidou is a Doubao car brand or evidence of the company entering the automotive manufacturing sector are inaccurate. […]
item.content:encoded:
ByteDance on Saturday denied recent speculation that it is developing its own vehicle or automotive brand, after some media reports linked the name Saidou to the company’s AI assistant Doubao. In a statement, ByteDance said claims that Saidou is a Doubao car brand or evidence of the company entering the automotive manufacturing sector are inaccurate. Saidou is neither a vehicle brand launched by ByteDance nor by Doubao, and ByteDance has no equity partnership with the company behind the brand, the company said.

ByteDance said its collaborations with automakers through Doubao and its cloud platform Volcano Engine focus on providing technologies such as Doubao large language models and smart cockpit solutions to enhance in-vehicle AI interactions and user experiences. [TechNode reporting]
